APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML conference scope: semantic parsing and structured prediction. The paper makes an explicitly relevant systems contribution (a transition set handling reentrancy, discontinuity, and non-terminals), evaluates with appropriate metrics (labeled precision/recall/F) on two UCCA corpora, compares against strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, Malt, Stack LSTM), and reports hyperparameters and training details (dynamic oracle, Adam). From a reproducibility-oriented perspective I would have liked explicit mention of released code and handling of discarded node types noted, but overall the experimental protocol and baselines are the right standard for this venue. | B(5): This paper fits squarely within a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it presents the first parser for a linguistically-motivated graph-based semantic formalism (UCCA) and introduces a general transition system handling reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals—issues of clear interest to the semantic parsing community. The work employs contemporary neural methods (BiLSTM feature extractor, dynamic oracle) and compares against strong baselines on standard UCCA corpora (Wiki, 20K Leagues) using appropriate labeled precision/recall/F metrics, so methods and evaluation meet venue standards. The paper is aimed at the right audience (semantic parsing/representation researchers) and is well positioned with thorough related work and realistic limitations discussed.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML conference scope: semantic parsing and structured prediction. The paper makes an explicitly relevant systems contribution (a transition set handling reentrancy, discontinuity, and non-terminals), evaluates with appropriate metrics (labeled precision/recall/F) on two UCCA corpora, compares against strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, Malt, Stack LSTM), and reports hyperparameters and training details (dynamic oracle, Adam). From a reproducibility-oriented perspective I would have liked explicit mention of released code and handling of discarded node types noted, but overall the experimental protocol and baselines are the right standard for this venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits squarely within a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it presents the first parser for a linguistically-motivated graph-based semantic formalism (UCCA) and introduces a general transition system handling reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals—issues of clear interest to the semantic parsing community. The work employs contemporary neural methods (BiLSTM feature extractor, dynamic oracle) and compares against strong baselines on standard UCCA corpora (Wiki, 20K Leagues) using appropriate labeled precision/recall/F metrics, so methods and evaluation meet venue standards. The paper is aimed at the right audience (semantic parsing/representation researchers) and is well positioned with thorough related work and realistic limitations discussed.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—parsing the UCCA semantic framework—and introduces the first dedicated parser (TUPA) that explicitly handles reentrancy, discontinuities, and non-terminal nodes, which are core challenges for semantic graph parsing. Empirically, TUPA (with a BiLSTM feature extractor and neural classifier) outperforms multiple strong baselines in both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, indicating substantive practical value. From a reproducibility perspective the paper reports hyperparameters and evaluation metrics on standard UCCA corpora, but the absence of an explicit statement about released code/models (per the summary) may limit immediate adoption and replication. Overall this is a strong contribution likely to change practice in UCCA parsing and stimulate follow-up work, though wider impact depends on making tooling and models available. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical and theoretical problem by delivering the first parser for UCCA, a semantic formalism that demands handling of reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminal nodes—capabilities many prior parsers lack. The combination of a novel transition set with a neural/BiLSTM classifier and strong empirical gains on in- and out-of-domain UCCA corpora demonstrates a clear advance that will change how researchers approach parsing of graph-based semantic representations. By operationalizing UCCA parsing and showing competitive performance versus multiple baselines, the work opens concrete new directions (robust graph transitions, dynamic oracles, cross-formalism comparisons) and is likely to be adopted and extended by researchers working on semantic graphs. Its impact is strong within semantic parsing and relevant to other graph-based meaning representations, though it is not yet clearly transformative across all of NLP.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—parsing the UCCA semantic framework—and introduces the first dedicated parser (TUPA) that explicitly handles reentrancy, discontinuities, and non-terminal nodes, which are core challenges for semantic graph parsing. Empirically, TUPA (with a BiLSTM feature extractor and neural classifier) outperforms multiple strong baselines in both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, indicating substantive practical value. From a reproducibility perspective the paper reports hyperparameters and evaluation metrics on standard UCCA corpora, but the absence of an explicit statement about released code/models (per the summary) may limit immediate adoption and replication. Overall this is a strong contribution likely to change practice in UCCA parsing and stimulate follow-up work, though wider impact depends on making tooling and models available.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical and theoretical problem by delivering the first parser for UCCA, a semantic formalism that demands handling of reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminal nodes—capabilities many prior parsers lack. The combination of a novel transition set with a neural/BiLSTM classifier and strong empirical gains on in- and out-of-domain UCCA corpora demonstrates a clear advance that will change how researchers approach parsing of graph-based semantic representations. By operationalizing UCCA parsing and showing competitive performance versus multiple baselines, the work opens concrete new directions (robust graph transitions, dynamic oracles, cross-formalism comparisons) and is likely to be adopted and extended by researchers working on semantic graphs. Its impact is strong within semantic parsing and relevant to other graph-based meaning representations, though it is not yet clearly transformative across all of NLP.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): From the summary, TUPA presents a clearly new practical solution: a transition-based parser with a novel transition set that explicitly handles reentrancy (multiple parents), discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes—capabilities not supported jointly by the compared baselines. The paper differentiates its contribution by coupling that transition set with modern neural classifiers and BiLSTM feature extractors, and evaluates on UCCA corpora against a range of parsers, which makes the technical advance a non-trivial extension of existing transition parsers. Given that it targets a less-studied formalism (UCCA) and claims to be the first dedicated parser for it, the work would be informative to experts, though it largely builds on known neural classifier and transition-parsing machinery rather than introducing radically new learning paradigms. | B(4): From the summary, the paper makes a clearly new contribution by presenting the first parser specifically targeting UCCA and by designing a transition set that explicitly handles multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuities, and non-terminal nodes—properties that standard dependency and constituency parsers do not cover. While the use of neural classifiers and BiLSTM feature extractors follows contemporary practice, the combination with a novel transition system and a dynamic oracle for this particular semantic formalism represents a non-trivial and well-differentiated advance over prior parsers (e.g., DAGParser, TurboParser, stack-LSTM approaches). This would be informative to experts working on graph-based semantic parsing, even if some components reuse known neural techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: From the summary, TUPA presents a clearly new practical solution: a transition-based parser with a novel transition set that explicitly handles reentrancy (multiple parents), discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes—capabilities not supported jointly by the compared baselines. The paper differentiates its contribution by coupling that transition set with modern neural classifiers and BiLSTM feature extractors, and evaluates on UCCA corpora against a range of parsers, which makes the technical advance a non-trivial extension of existing transition parsers. Given that it targets a less-studied formalism (UCCA) and claims to be the first dedicated parser for it, the work would be informative to experts, though it largely builds on known neural classifier and transition-parsing machinery rather than introducing radically new learning paradigms.

reviewer_b.justification: From the summary, the paper makes a clearly new contribution by presenting the first parser specifically targeting UCCA and by designing a transition set that explicitly handles multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuities, and non-terminal nodes—properties that standard dependency and constituency parsers do not cover. While the use of neural classifiers and BiLSTM feature extractors follows contemporary practice, the combination with a novel transition system and a dynamic oracle for this particular semantic formalism represents a non-trivial and well-differentiated advance over prior parsers (e.g., DAGParser, TurboParser, stack-LSTM approaches). This would be informative to experts working on graph-based semantic parsing, even if some components reuse known neural techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing TUPA (with BiLSTM features) outperforms multiple strong baselines on in- and out-of-domain UCCA parsing; methodology details such as the novel transition set, dynamic oracle, optimizer, and hyperparameters are reported, enabling reproduction. However, key reproducibility aspects are missing or weak: public code/models are not mentioned, and there is no indication of reporting variance across random seeds or statistical significance testing. The authors acknowledge relevant limitations (discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion upper bounds), which mitigates some concerns, so the technical contribution appears sound though minor experimental-rigour gaps remain. | B(4): The paper's main claims—that TUPA handles reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals and that a BiLSTM-based variant outperforms strong baselines on UCCA—are supported by experiments on the Wiki and 20K Leagues corpora using appropriate labeled precision/recall/F metrics and multiple competitive baselines. The transition set, dynamic oracle training and neural classifiers are technically plausible and no mathematical derivations were required beyond algorithmic descriptions. Minor methodological concerns remain (training/evaluation omits inter-sentence, linkage and implicit nodes; conversion to bilexical graphs creates an acknowledged upper bound for baselines; no mention of statistical significance testing), but these do not invalidate the main experimental findings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing TUPA (with BiLSTM features) outperforms multiple strong baselines on in- and out-of-domain UCCA parsing; methodology details such as the novel transition set, dynamic oracle, optimizer, and hyperparameters are reported, enabling reproduction. However, key reproducibility aspects are missing or weak: public code/models are not mentioned, and there is no indication of reporting variance across random seeds or statistical significance testing. The authors acknowledge relevant limitations (discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion upper bounds), which mitigates some concerns, so the technical contribution appears sound though minor experimental-rigour gaps remain.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims—that TUPA handles reentrancy, discontinuities and non-terminals and that a BiLSTM-based variant outperforms strong baselines on UCCA—are supported by experiments on the Wiki and 20K Leagues corpora using appropriate labeled precision/recall/F metrics and multiple competitive baselines. The transition set, dynamic oracle training and neural classifiers are technically plausible and no mathematical derivations were required beyond algorithmic descriptions. Minor methodological concerns remain (training/evaluation omits inter-sentence, linkage and implicit nodes; conversion to bilexical graphs creates an acknowledged upper bound for baselines; no mention of statistical significance testing), but these do not invalidate the main experimental findings.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broadly comprehensive: TUPA is evaluated in-domain and out-of-domain against multiple strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack LSTM) and reports labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges. The paper includes useful ablations (sparse linear, MLP, BiLSTM classifiers) and reports hyperparameters and training details (dynamic oracle, Adam), but I see limited sensitivity analyses (e.g., variance across seeds, beam/decoding settings) and no explicit mention of released code for exact reproduction. Related work is well covered and several limitations are acknowledged (discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes; conversion upper bounds), so overall the empirical treatment is strong but missing a few reproducibility and robustness analyses. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is solid: TUPA is compared to multiple strong baselines on two UCCA corpora with in-domain and out-of-domain results, and different classifier variants (sparse linear, MLP, BiLSTM) act as useful ablations showing the benefit of the BiLSTM feature extractor. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the paper explicitly discusses limitations (discarding inter-sentence, linkage, and implicit nodes) and conversion upper bounds for baseline comparisons. Reproducibility is mostly supported by reported hyperparameters, training details (dynamic oracle, Adam), and evaluation metrics, but the absence of an explicit code release mention and some preprocessing choices (e.g., discarded node types) leave minor gaps for full replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are broadly comprehensive: TUPA is evaluated in-domain and out-of-domain against multiple strong baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack LSTM) and reports labeled precision/recall/F for primary and remote edges. The paper includes useful ablations (sparse linear, MLP, BiLSTM classifiers) and reports hyperparameters and training details (dynamic oracle, Adam), but I see limited sensitivity analyses (e.g., variance across seeds, beam/decoding settings) and no explicit mention of released code for exact reproduction. Related work is well covered and several limitations are acknowledged (discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes; conversion upper bounds), so overall the empirical treatment is strong but missing a few reproducibility and robustness analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is solid: TUPA is compared to multiple strong baselines on two UCCA corpora with in-domain and out-of-domain results, and different classifier variants (sparse linear, MLP, BiLSTM) act as useful ablations showing the benefit of the BiLSTM feature extractor. Related work appears thoroughly covered and the paper explicitly discusses limitations (discarding inter-sentence, linkage, and implicit nodes) and conversion upper bounds for baseline comparisons. Reproducibility is mostly supported by reported hyperparameters, training details (dynamic oracle, Adam), and evaluation metrics, but the absence of an explicit code release mention and some preprocessing choices (e.g., discarded node types) leave minor gaps for full replication.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper appears well organized and readable: the contributions (TUPA and its neural variants) are stated early, the method section describes the novel transition set and classifier variants, and experiments report clear metrics (precision/recall/F) on Wiki and 20K Leagues with reported hyperparameters. Notation and writing are described as good and related work coverage is thorough, though the summary does not mention the presence or quality of figures/tables and some preprocessing choices (discarding inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes, conversion upper bounds) could benefit from slightly clearer exposition. From a reproducibility-oriented perspective the paper conveys the necessary experimental details, but would be improved by explicit calls-out of released code and visual aids to illustrate the transition set and conversions. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation through method and experiments, and the main contributions (TUPA’s novel transition set and neural variants) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate UCCA-specific phenomena (reentrancy, discontinuities, non-terminals). Writing is generally good and readable, though a few sections (e.g., conversion-to-bilexical baselines and evaluation upper bounds) could be clarified further for readers less familiar with parsing formalisms.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper appears well organized and readable: the contributions (TUPA and its neural variants) are stated early, the method section describes the novel transition set and classifier variants, and experiments report clear metrics (precision/recall/F) on Wiki and 20K Leagues with reported hyperparameters. Notation and writing are described as good and related work coverage is thorough, though the summary does not mention the presence or quality of figures/tables and some preprocessing choices (discarding inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes, conversion upper bounds) could benefit from slightly clearer exposition. From a reproducibility-oriented perspective the paper conveys the necessary experimental details, but would be improved by explicit calls-out of released code and visual aids to illustrate the transition set and conversions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation through method and experiments, and the main contributions (TUPA’s novel transition set and neural variants) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate UCCA-specific phenomena (reentrancy, discontinuities, non-terminals). Writing is generally good and readable, though a few sections (e.g., conversion-to-bilexical baselines and evaluation upper bounds) could be clarified further for readers less familiar with parsing formalisms.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares TUPA against a broad set of appropriate and recent baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack LSTM) using the same UCCA datasets and the same labeled P/R/F evaluation reported separately for primary and remote edges. The authors explicitly note important evaluation caveats (discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion-induced upper bounds), and report hyperparameters and variants (sparse/MLP/BiLSTM), which supports fair comparison. However, from the summary it is not clear whether statistical significance or variance across seeds is reported or whether cases where prior methods match or outperform TUPA are analyzed in depth, so the comparisons are strong but miss some robustness and error-analysis details. | B(5): The paper evaluates TUPA against a broad set of strong and relevant baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack LSTM) on the same UCCA corpora using standard labeled precision/recall/F1 for primary and remote edges. The authors explicitly handle evaluation confounds—reporting the effect of converting to bilexical graphs (upper bound due to removal of non-terminals) and noting discarded linkage/implicit/inter-sentence edges—so comparisons are reported fairly and transparently. Prior methods that perform comparably or better are acknowledged and differences (e.g., handling of reentrancy, discontinuities, non-terminals) are clearly discussed, and related work coverage is described as thorough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares TUPA against a broad set of appropriate and recent baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack LSTM) using the same UCCA datasets and the same labeled P/R/F evaluation reported separately for primary and remote edges. The authors explicitly note important evaluation caveats (discarded inter-sentence/linkage/implicit nodes and conversion-induced upper bounds), and report hyperparameters and variants (sparse/MLP/BiLSTM), which supports fair comparison. However, from the summary it is not clear whether statistical significance or variance across seeds is reported or whether cases where prior methods match or outperform TUPA are analyzed in depth, so the comparisons are strong but miss some robustness and error-analysis details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates TUPA against a broad set of strong and relevant baselines (DAGParser, TurboParser, UPARSE, MaltParser, Stack LSTM) on the same UCCA corpora using standard labeled precision/recall/F1 for primary and remote edges. The authors explicitly handle evaluation confounds—reporting the effect of converting to bilexical graphs (upper bound due to removal of non-terminals) and noting discarded linkage/implicit/inter-sentence edges—so comparisons are reported fairly and transparently. Prior methods that perform comparably or better are acknowledged and differences (e.g., handling of reentrancy, discontinuities, non-terminals) are clearly discussed, and related work coverage is described as thorough.

main_claims: We present TUPA, the first parser for the UCCA semantic representation.
TUPA is a general transition-based parser that supports multiple parents (reentrancy), discontinuous units, and non-terminal nodes.
When coupled with a neural classifier and BiLSTM feature extractor, TUPA outperforms a variety of strong baselines on UCCA parsing in both in-domain and out-of-domain settings.
_summary.methodology: A transition-based parser with a novel transition set, using a neural network classifier (variants include sparse linear, MLP, and BiLSTM-based models) and BiLSTM-based feature extraction; trained with a dynamic oracle and optimized with Adam.
datasets: UCCA Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea English-French parallel corpus (20K Leagues)
metrics: Labeled F-score (on edges), reported separately for primary and remote edges
limitations: Training and evaluation are performed on individual sentences after discarding inter-sentence edges (0.18% of edges).
Linkage nodes/edges and implicit nodes are discarded from training and evaluation.
Conversion to bilexical graphs/trees (for comparison baselines) induces an upper bound on achievable scores due to removal of non-terminal nodes.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Systems Engineer
reviewer_a.background: ML/NLP engineer with a PhD-level background in parsing and 8+ years building, evaluating, and deploying structured prediction systems; experience implementing transition-based parsers and reproducible evaluation pipelines.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocols, hyperparameter settings, and training/dev/test splits to ensure reproducibility
looks for thorough baselines and ablation studies that isolate the contribution of model components (e.g., transition set vs classifier vs feature extractor)
evaluates statistical rigor: significance testing, variance across seeds, and robustness to domain shift
checks implementation details that affect fairness (oracle, beam size, decoding strategy, handling of unknown words and punctuation)
values publicly released code, data preprocessing scripts, and trained models for replication
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the methods are described and evaluated rigorously enough to support the empirical claims and to allow others to reproduce the results.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic Parsing Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in semantic representation and parsing with extensive publications on graph-based formalisms (AMR, SDP, UCCA) and experience assessing theoretical expressiveness and practical utility of meaning representations.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior semantic parsing work and how the approach advances handling of reentrancy, discontinuities, and non-terminals
values clear motivation for why the chosen representation and parser design matter for downstream tasks or linguistic coverage
notices connections and contrasts to alternative formalisms (AMR, SDP, constituency) and prior parsing strategies (transition-based, graph-based, neural encoders)
cares about impact: dataset coverage, generality of the approach across languages/domains, and potential to catalyze further research or applications
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's conceptual contribution and placement within the semantic parsing literature, weighing novelty, relevance, and potential impact on the field.
